macro_segments.title: Rav Anan's Refusal of Gifts and the Principle of Avoiding Bribery in Judgment

micro_segments.title: Story of Rav Anan rejecting a basket of fish and refusing to judge the litigant
The principle that gifts to Torah sages are like bringing bikkurim
Discussion of the substitute for aliyah laregel: receiving the rebbe
Rav Anan accepts the gift after the litigant explains its spiritual purpose

macro_segments.title: The Consequences of Rav Anan's Actions: Rav Nachman's Bias and the Silencing of the Litigant

micro_segments.title: Rav Anan sends the litigant to Rav Nachman with a letter explaining his recusal
Rav Nachman misinterprets the letter as indicating the litigant is Rav Anan's relative
Rav Nachman prioritizes the orphans' case versus the duty to honor Torah through Rav Anan
Rav Nachman abandons the orphans' case to hear the litigant first, creating the appearance of bias
The litigant is silenced and unable to present his case freely due to perceived favoritism
Teaching on how judicial actions communicate bias even when unintentional, with real-world examples

macro_segments.title: Rav Anan's Punishment and Eliyahu's Withdrawal: Divine Consequence and Reconciliation

micro_segments.title: Rav Anan's regular visitations from Eliyahu and the teaching of Seder de-Eliyahu
Eliyahu withdraws from Rav Anan after the incident with the letter and gift
Rav Anan fasts and prays for mercy; Eliyahu returns but Rav Anan is afraid
Rav Anan sits in a box while Eliyahu completes teaching him; origin of Seder Eliyahu Rabba and Zuta
Explanation of why there are two versions: larger teaching before the box, smaller teaching within
Maharsha's explanation: fasting atones for accepting the gift; trembling mirrors the litigant's fear

macro_segments.title: Rav Yosef and the Question of Prayer During Famine: Comparison to Elisha's Times

micro_segments.title: In Rav Yosef's time there was divine anger (famine); rabbis ask him to pray for relief
Rav Yosef compares to Elisha: in Elisha's time there was anger but he did not pray
Question: How do we know Elisha had 2,200 students from the verse about bread for 100 people?
Calculation: 22 loaves of bread divided among 100 people each equals 2,200 people total

macro_segments.title: Decline of the Yeshivas from Elisha to Later Generations

micro_segments.title: Students remaining in the yeshiva of Rav: 1,200 rabbis
Students remaining in the yeshiva of Rav Huna: 800 rabbis; Rav Huna taught with 13 amoraim
When students left Rav Huna's yeshiva, the dust from their garments would cover the sun
Students remaining in the yeshiva of Rava and Rav Yosef: 400 rabbis; they called themselves orphans
Students remaining in the yeshiva of Abaye or Rav Pappa or Rav Ashi: 200 rabbis; orphans of orphans

macro_segments.title: Payment from Communal Funds for Temple Services and Related Personnel

micro_segments.title: Inspectors of blemishes in Jerusalem received payment from terumat halishka
Torah sages teaching kohanim the laws of shechita received payment from terumat halishka
Torah sages teaching kohanim the laws of kemitza received payment from terumat halishka
Those proofreading Torah scrolls in Jerusalem received payment from terumat halishka
Women weaving the parochet (curtain) received payment; discussion of whether it is bedek habayit or not
Explanation of thirteen parochet curtains: seven for gates, one for heichal, one for ulam, two in dvir with two above

macro_segments.title: Sources of Funds for Various Temple Services: Bedek Habayit vs. Terumat Halishka

micro_segments.title: Women raising children for the para aduma service received payment from terumat halishka
Question about whether vessels (klei shares) come from bedek habayit or terumat halishka
Answer: vessels come from terumat halishka because they serve the korban
Proof from Divrei Hayamim: leftover money for bedek habayit was used to make vessels
Parallel from Melachim: money for bedek habayit should not go to vessels but to craftsmen
Resolution: one case had leftover funds, the other did not; with leftovers, beit din stipulates reassignment

macro_segments.title: The Tannaitic Debate on Sources for Vessels and Altar Maintenance

micro_segments.title: Tana debei Rabbi Yishmael: vessels come from terumat halishka, proved from 'sheir hakesef'
Alternative reading: maybe vessels come from shirei (leftovers) of terumat halishka
Response: hakesef refers to the money itself, not the remainder; kesef rishon is the main fund
List of items coming from terumat halishka: ketores, communal sacrifices, golden altar, vessels
Alternative sources: mizbeach hazahav and vessels from motar nesachim (excess libation money)
Mizbeach ha'ola, chambers, courtyards come from bedek habayit; outside walls from shirei lishka
Walls of Jerusalem and city needs come from shirei halishka

macro_segments.title: Tannaitic Opinions on Use of Leftover Funds: Profit-Making vs. Dignified Use

micro_segments.title: First opinion: leftover truma used for 'kayitz hamizbeach' - keeping the altar busy with offerings
Business model: buy fruit cheaply, sell expensively; profits go to kayitz hamizbeach
Rabbi Yishmael: buy wines, oils, and fine flour; profits go to Temple expenses
Rabbi Akiva: Hekdesh money should not be used for profit; disrespectful to holy funds
Further objection by Rabbi Akiva: poor people's money should not be invested; they need it immediately

topical_tags.term: lev beit din matne aleihem (beit din stipulation)
